weeks.weekly_summary: Foundation week with 2 runs on preferred days (Monday, Wednesday). Focus on easy aerobic running to establish base and adapt to training schedule.
weeks.coaching_notes: Recent activity shows ~10 km/week; ramping to 16 km respects the 4-hour weekly budget while building aerobic base. 1 quality session (fartlek) on Wednesday introduces light intensity variation without excessive fatigue. Athlete is fresh—good time to establish rhythm on Mon/Wed pattern.
weeks.next_week_preview: Week 2 builds volume to ~20 km and introduces tempo work. Continue Mon/Wed structure with progressive intensity.

days.title: 🏋️ Gym
days.description: Strength and conditioning session. Focus on core, legs, and upper body stability for running.
days.coach_analysis: You nailed the cross-training session—nearly 49 minutes of focused strength work with a solid avg HR of 111 bpm shows good intensity without overdoing it. This is exactly the kind of session that builds running resilience; the core and leg stability work will pay dividends in your next few runs. Recovery-wise, you're in good shape—nothing here that should compromise your mid-week training.

days.title: ⚽ Football
days.description: Football match or training. Light-to-moderate effort acceptable during Base Building phase.

days.title: Rest Day
days.description: Active recovery. Walk, stretch, or easy mobility work.

days.title: Easy 8 km
days.description: Conversational-pace aerobic run. Establish Monday rhythm. Focus on form and breathing cadence. Flat course preferred.
days.notes: First run of training block on preferred day

days.title: 🧘 Pilates
days.description: Pilates session for core stability and flexibility. Complement running with controlled movements.
days.notes: Fixed commitment—blocked day. Light enough for day before Wednesday quality

days.title: Fartlek: Easy with Surges (8 km total)
days.description: 5 min easy warm-up, then 6×1 min at tempo effort (Zone 3, ~4:50/km) with 1 min recovery jog between. 3 min easy cool-down. Builds aerobic power and introduces intensity without shock loading.
days.notes: Quality session #1 of week. Use landmarks or music to pace surges. Keep recoveries truly easy.

days.title: Rest Day
days.description: Full rest or very light mobility work (foam roll, yoga). Prepare for next week.

weeks.weekly_summary: Volume increases to ~20 km (within 4-hour budget). Tempo work introduced on Wednesday. Continue Mon/Wed rhythm with progressive difficulty.
weeks.coaching_notes: Week 2 pushes volume +25% from Week 1 while maintaining polarization. Monday easy run increases to 9 km; Wednesday tempo builds aerobic threshold. No overtraining flags—athlete recent activity shows readiness for progression. Tempo session bridges to Aerobic Development phase.
weeks.next_week_preview: Week 3 maintains ~22 km volume with progressive long run (up to 10+ km). Tempo on Wednesday evolves into longer sustained efforts.

days.title: 🏋️ Gym
days.description: Strength session. Lower body focus (squats, lunges, calf raises) for running power.

days.title: ⚽ Football
days.description: Football training or match. Moderate effort acceptable.

days.title: Rest Day
days.description: Active recovery. Stretch or light walk.

days.title: Easy 9 km
days.description: Steady easy run on flat terrain. Conversational pace. Build aerobic volume.

days.title: 🧘 Pilates
days.description: Pilates for core and flexibility.

days.title: Tempo: 2×5 min at HM Pace (8 km total)
days.description: 3 min warm-up easy, then 5 min at half-marathon pace (~5:00/km, comfortably hard), 2 min recovery jog, repeat 5 min tempo, 3 min cool-down. Builds lactate threshold.
days.notes: Quality session #2. Target tempo effort: slightly hard but sustainable for full 5 min.

days.title: Rest Day
days.description: Rest or gentle mobility.

weeks.weekly_summary: Volume increases to ~22 km as training transitions into Aerobic Development. Long run introduced Sunday (9 km). Tempo work continues Wednesday with progression toward sustained efforts.
weeks.coaching_notes: Week 3 introduces long-run stimulus (Sunday 9 km) while maintaining Mon/Wed quality structure. Tempo on Wednesday extends to 3×3 min, building toward 15-20 min sustained efforts in Week 4. Long run is easy; maintains aerobic focus. Progression is gradual and sustainable.
weeks.next_week_preview: Week 4 (final week of block) caps volume at ~24 km with long run building to 10+ km and tempo evolving to 20+ min sustained. Prepare for next 4-week Aerobic Development block.

days.title: 🏋️ Gym
days.description: Strength session. Focus on glutes, hamstrings, and core stability.

days.title: ⚽ Football
days.description: Football session. Moderate intensity.

days.title: Long Run: Easy 9 km
days.description: Sunday long run at easy pace. Build aerobic capacity and running economy. No fast segments. Conversational pace throughout. Walk/jog as needed to stay easy.
days.notes: First long run of block. Set foundation for longer distances ahead.

days.title: Easy 5 km (recovery after long run)
days.description: Short, easy recovery run following Sunday long run. Keep HR low. Focus on movement quality and aerobic recovery.
days.notes: Reduced Monday volume due to Sunday long run.

days.title: 🧘 Pilates
days.description: Pilates for core and hip mobility. Light complement to easy Monday.

days.title: Tempo Intervals: 3×3 min at HM Pace (8 km total)
days.description: 3 min easy warm-up, then 3 reps of 3 min at half-marathon pace (~5:00/km), 2 min easy recovery jog between reps, 2 min cool-down. Accumulates 9 min total at tempo effort.
days.notes: Quality session #3. Build toward sustained 15-20 min efforts in coming weeks.

days.title: Rest Day
days.description: Full rest. Prepare for final week of block.

weeks.weekly_summary: Final week of 4-week block builds to peak volume (~24 km) with long run at 10+ km and sustained tempo work. Maintains Monday/Wednesday structure; sets foundation for next training block.
weeks.coaching_notes: Week 4 culminates block progression: long run increases to 10 km (Sunday); tempo shifts to sustained 15 min effort (Wednesday). Volume reaches ~24 km—highest of block while respecting 4-hour weekly budget. Athlete has built solid aerobic base; ready for next block focused on peak intensity and race-specific work. No fatigue flags anticipated.
weeks.next_week_preview: Upon completion, athlete enters 12-week build toward half-marathon race. Next 4-week block (Aerobic Development Phase 2) will introduce long runs of 11-12 km and longer tempo efforts (20+ min). Quality session frequency remains 1/week.

days.title: 🏋️ Gym
days.description: Strength session. Maintain lower body and core work.

days.title: ⚽ Football
days.description: Football. Moderate intensity.

days.title: Long Run: Easy 10 km
days.description: Peak long run of block at easy pace. 10 km at conversational pace. Strengthen aerobic engine and mental resilience. Keep HR controlled; focus on even pacing. Walk breaks acceptable if needed to maintain easy effort.
days.notes: +1 km from Week 3 long run. Prepare for 11-12 km long runs in next block.

days.title: Easy 5 km (recovery)
days.description: Short, easy recovery run after Sunday long run. Low intensity, high cadence, strong form focus.

days.title: 🧘 Pilates
days.description: Pilates session. Maintain mobility and core strength.

days.title: Sustained Tempo: 15 min at HM Pace (9 km total)
days.description: 4 min easy warm-up, then 15 min continuous at half-marathon pace (~5:00/km, comfortably hard), 3 min easy cool-down. Tests lactate threshold and race-pace sustainability.
days.notes: Quality session #4. Peak intensity of block. This sustained effort is cornerstone of HM fitness development. Effort should feel hard but sustainable—should be able to speak single words but not sentences.

days.title: Rest Day
days.description: Full rest. End-of-block recovery. Reflect on progress; prepare for next training phase.
days.notes: End of 4-week Base Building/Aerobic Development block.

coaching_overview:
This 4-week Base Building → Aerobic Development block establishes the foundation for your half-marathon goal (2:00, ~5:40/km pace). You train exclusively on Monday and Wednesday (respecting your 2-sessions/week and time budget of 4 hours), with cross-training (gym, football, pilates) on fixed days (Fri, Sat, Tue) and Sunday reserved for the long run in Weeks 3–4.

**Week 1–2 (Base Building):** Ramp volume from 16 → 20 km. Introduce light fartlek (Week 1) and tempo intervals (Week 2) at Zone 3 to build lactate threshold awareness without heavy fatigue. Easy Monday runs establish rhythm and consistency.

**Week 3–4 (Aerobic Development):** Long runs debut Sunday at 9 km (Week 3) → 10 km (Week 4). Wednesday tempo progresses from 2×5 min → 3×3 min → 15 min sustained, teaching your aerobic system to sustain half-marathon pace. Monday runs drop to 5 km post-long-run for active recovery.

**Polarized 80/20 Structure:** ~80% of running is easy Zone 2 aerobic work; ~20% is quality (tempo/fartlek) at Zone 3. This minimizes junk-mile fatigue while maximizing adaptation.

**Key Adaptation Signals:** Your recent activity (9.9 km / 1 hour over 14 days) shows you're not heavily trained. This plan ramps progressively (16 → 20 → 22 → 24 km/week) to avoid overtraining. Monitor HRV and perceived fatigue; if fatigued after Week 2, extend recovery or reduce Wednesday intensity.

**Next Block:** After Week 4, enter 12-week Aerobic Development + Peak Training phases. Expect long runs to grow to 11–13 km, tempo to extend to 20–25 min sustained, and introduction of VO2 max intervals closer to race.

**Total 4-Week Distance:** 82 km across 8 runs + 12 cross-training sessions. You'll arrive at Week 5 with solid aerobic base, tempo fitness at half-marathon pace, and readiness for race-specific training.
